analogy: Think of "Winds Storms and Cyclones" through the chapter's own opening images: Reproduction in Plants8 T o produce its kind is a characteristic of all living organisms. The production of new individuals from their parents is known as reproduction. There are different modes of reproduction in plants which we shall learn in this chapter. 8.1 MODES OF... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Winds Storms and Cyclones is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that reproduction in Plants8 T o produce its kind is a characteristic of all living organisms.

The text explains that the production of new individuals from their parents is known as reproduction.

The text explains that there are different modes of reproduction in plants which we shall learn in this chapter.

The text explains that 8.1 MODES OF REPRODUCTION In Class VI you learnt about different parts of a flowering plant.

The text explains that try to list the various parts of a plant and write the functions of each.

The text explains that these are called the vegetative parts of a plant.

The text explains that after a certain period of growth, most plants bear flowers.

The text explains that you may have seen the mango trees flowering in spring.

The text explains that it is these flowers that give rise to juicy mango fruit we enjoy in summer.

The text explains that we eat the fruits and usually discard the seeds.

The text explains that so, what is the function of flowers in plants?

The text explains that flowers perform the function of reproduction in plants.

The text explains that there are several ways by which plants produce their offspring.

The text explains that these are categorised into two types: (i) asexual, and (ii) sexual reproduction.

The text explains that in asexual reproduction plants can give rise to new plants without seeds, whereas in sexual Asexual reproduction In asexual reproduction new plants are obtained without production of seeds.

The text explains that vegetative propagation It is a type of asexual reproduction in which new plants are produced from roots, stems, leaves and buds.

The text explains that each spore is covered by a hard protective coat to withstand unfavourable conditions such as high temperature and low humidity.

The text explains that there would be severe competition for sunlight, water, minerals and space.

The text explains that it prevents competition between the plant and its own seedlings for sunlight, water and minerals.

The text explains that zygote develops into an embryo.  Fruit is the mature ovary whereas ovule develops into a seed, which contains the developing embryo.  Seed dispersal is aided by wind, water and animals.  Seed dispersal helps the plants to (i) prevent overcrowding, (ii) avoid competition for sunlight, water and minerals and (iii) invade new habitats.

The text explains that if many fruits are not available, you can collect tomatoes and cucumbers (these are fruits, though we use them as vegetables).

The text explains that split the fruits and examine the seeds within.

The text explains that look for any special characteristics in the fruits and their seeds.

The text explains that you can visit a library also to learn about this.

The text explains that remember that many vegetables are also fruits of the plants.

The text explains that discuss with your teacher, parents, farmers, fruit growers and agricultural experts (if available nearby) and find out the manner of their dispersal.

The text explains that present your data in the form of a table as shown below: 4.

The text explains that suppose there is one member of a particular kind of organism in a culture dish, which doubles itself in one hour through asexual reproduction.

The text explains that work out the number of members of that kind of organism which will be present in the culture dish after ten hours.

The text explains that such a colony of individuals arising from one parent is called a “clone”.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 8: Winds Storms and Cyclones

prelims_traps: Do not treat 8.1 MODES OF REPRODUCTION and R EPRODUCTION IN PLANTS 83 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Winds Storms and Cyclones; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Winds Storms and Cyclones through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 8.1 MODES OF REPRODUCTION and R EPRODUCTION IN PLANTS 83 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
